# Questions in Livestream 225

Live Stream #225: Ancient Egyptian Pyramid Scheme

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=560" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:20</a> | Could the Israelites have invented an Exodus story while already living in Canaan? | He says that would be an odd and self-damaging story to invent there, so he does not find the objection persuasive. | He argues that if a people were inventing an origin story, they would normally make themselves sound noble, as in other ancient national myths. He adds that critical scholars often date the Song of the Sea very early, near the period in question, which makes a late, arbitrary invention less plausible to him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=1005" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:45</a> | If your life had an anime intro, what song would it use? | He answers playfully with a music pick rather than a technical discussion. | He says he has never found an anime theme that fits his life, joking that his life is too strange even for anime. He considers themes from *Knights of Sidonia*, *Black Lagoon*, *Wings of Honneamise*, and *Orange Road*, but ends by saying he cannot pick one. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=1191" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:51</a> | Are there archaeological discoveries that suggest God specifically preserved them for the modern world? | He says some finds can feel providential, but he avoids claiming private certainty about specific artifacts. | He says he believes in providence, including general providence, but does not like arguing that particular finds were preserved just for this age. For him, the stronger point is that the little archaeology already recovered implies much more evidence remains in the ground, though funding, access, and resources limit what can be found. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=1282" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:22</a> | How much Egyptian material is still buried, or what percentage has been excavated? | He says the vast majority is still unexcavated and only a small fraction has been recovered. | He estimates that about 5% has been dug up and roughly 95% of Egyptian civilization remains buried. He connects that estimate to satellite mapping work that identified buried sites and says the situation may be even more limited in Israel, where comparable excavation and mapping coverage is not available. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=1456" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:16</a> | Is it possible that still-buried data could debunk a biblical story? | He says buried evidence can always force revision, but that is not the same thing as assuming the Bible will be disproved. | He says one must always be open to discoveries that challenge expectations, but the first question is whether the biblical text was wrong or whether the interpreter misread it. He uses Shishak as an example: archaeology pushed scholars back to the text, where Jerusalem is not actually listed as conquered but appears to have avoided conquest by payment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=1898" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:38</a> | Has reading a lot of literature deepened your understanding of your own imaginative process? | He says yes, wide reading sharpened his sense of how stories and imaginative worlds are built. | He actually answers "no" with respect to understanding his own imaginative process, while saying literature has deepened his thinking in many other ways. He stresses that writers need to read widely and practice the craft, but he finds the imaginative process itself difficult to explain and says existing writing books often handle craft better than imagination. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=2242" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:22</a> | Which classical Christian writings or authors would you recommend? | He recommends working through major classical Christian writers rather than staying only with modern summaries. | He says he generally does not recommend classic fiction, with a few exceptions, because older works can be out of touch with modern problems. He prefers that people read good living writers, especially current Christian or indie authors, while allowing that people who love classic fiction can still read it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=3173" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:53</a> | Do the "bulls of Bashan" imply Bashan had large wild bulls roaming there? | He says yes, the phrase reflects Bashan's reputation for notable cattle or bulls. | He identifies Bashan with a region including the Golan Heights and Syria and connects the phrase to aurochs, large wild cattle known for being huge and difficult to tame. He says they ranged across Turkey, Syria, and into Europe, and notes that they survived until relatively recently, going extinct around the 16th century AD. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=3276" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:36</a> | Did early Christians or Jews think Jonah was swallowed by a sea dragon? | He says some later readers moved in that direction, but the biblical text itself only requires a great sea creature. | He says the text only says Jonah was swallowed by a fish, and that "fish" is a broad category in the Israelite way of classifying animals. Since anything with fins that lived in the water could be called a fish, he leaves open whether it was a sea monster, whale, or some other creature, but says the text does not specify. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=3834" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:54</a> | Did manna fungus grow daily for 40 years? | He says it probably grew sporadically in the savannah-like Sinai conditions rather than as ordinary daily growth. | He says the Sinai at the time of the Exodus was savannah-like, with intermittent and seasonal rains, so a desert-truffle-like fungus could have appeared more often in wet periods and less in dry ones. He compares the biblical description of manna appearing with dew at dawn to the way some mushrooms fruit quickly in the morning and then decay within hours. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=4008" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:48</a> | Would manna have been something the Israelites had no experience with in Egypt, and was fungus common there? | He says fungus and mushrooms were not common in Egypt's agricultural setting, so the Israelites likely would not have known this kind of desert fungus there. | He says mushrooms were not really a feature of Egypt's agricultural black land because annual flooding and new soil prevented fungal colonies from establishing over years. He adds that Egypt's desert had not been savannah-like since the Old Kingdom, so by the Israelite period desert truffles would not have been a familiar Egyptian food. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=4115" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:35</a> | If manna had a natural analogue, how could it not grow on the Sabbath? | He says the natural process could still be under God's control, so the Sabbath pattern was God withholding or directing the blessing. | He says a natural mechanism and divine control are not mutually exclusive. As with the wind parting the Reed Sea, God can govern ordinary natural processes, so manna may be naturally occurring while God still "turns off the tap" or withholds blessing on the Sabbath. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=4949" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:29</a> | Why was salt included in the grain offering? | He ties it to covenantal permanence and sacrificial symbolism rather than ordinary seasoning. | He reads Leviticus 2:13 and says salt is both a preservative and a purification agent. In the grain offering it represents the covenant preserving the worshiper, and it also purifies what is offered, whether burned on the altar or given to the priests. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=5161" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:01</a> | How does the Egyptian ideal of living 110 years relate to Joseph dying at 110? | He says Joseph's age signals a full and honored life in Egyptian cultural terms. | He says 110 years functioned in Egypt as a symbolic or honorific age for important people who lived a full life, including magicians, scribes, and priests. He cites the magician Djedi in Papyrus Westcar as a parallel and says Joseph's age marks him as honored in Egyptian terms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=5462" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:02</a> | Is it possible to reconcile the death accounts of King Ahaziah in Kings and Chronicles? | He says a reconciliation is possible through chronology and regnal details rather than treating them as flat contradiction. | He focuses first on 2 Kings and says the Hebrew word often translated "there" is vague enough to mean something like "over there" rather than the exact spot just named. That allows Kings' account of Ahaziah fleeing toward Megiddo and dying "there" to be read less rigidly before comparing it with Chronicles. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=6225" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:45</a> | Are all spirits opposing God considered demons, and are all demons fallen angels? | He says no; demon, angel, and other hostile spirit categories should not be collapsed into one group. | He describes both "angel" and "demon" as job descriptions rather than species: angels are messengers, while demons are lower spirits doing tasks for evil powers. He distinguishes demons from principalities, powers, watchers, and other fallen spirits, and says not every demon should be treated as a fallen angel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=6484" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:04</a> | Would you do a Mr. E.C. show on models of the atonement? | He says it would make a worthwhile topic if time permits. | He says the idea is appealing and Mrs. Producer likes it, but he is unsure whether he can do it well because atonement models are complex. A Mr. E.C. episode has to compress many theories into a few minutes, so he worries that too much nuance could be lost. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=6635" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:35</a> | What books or resources help with ancient Near Eastern customs, idioms, and cultural context? | He recommends broad contextual study and reference works instead of relying on one shortcut source. | He says he does not have a single resource for idioms or figures of speech and usually goes to his commentary library. For ancient Near Eastern and Israelite religious customs, he specifically recommends Richard Hess, especially work on ancient Israelite religion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=6726" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:06</a> | Why were gardens in the ancient Near East associated with temples? | He says gardens symbolized ordered fertility and sacred space, so temple complexes naturally drew that imagery. | He says temples often had gardens outside them and gives examples from Deir el-Medina and early Egyptian temple settings with trees and even animals. The garden or animal area functioned as an earthly paradise through which one moved toward the temple's celestial or sacred realm. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=7188" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:48</a> | Do you do speaking engagements? | He says yes on occasion, depending on the topic and schedule. | He says he does do speaking engagements and had one coming up in October, though he is not invited very often. He adds that people ask him to do YouTube appearances more frequently, and that being in Canada may make in-person invitations less common. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=7293" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:33</a> | In Genesis 18:1-15, did God himself visit Abraham, and if so why was no sacred space required? | He treats it as a genuine divine appearance and says the oaks of Mamre already functioned as sacred space. | He reads Genesis 18 as saying the Lord appeared to Abraham by the oaks of Mamre, and he identifies the oak grove itself as sacred space. In his explanation, ancient Levantine oak groves could be ritual places, so Abraham did not have to create a sacred space because one was already present. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=7698" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:18</a> | Was Abraham's meal for the visitors a version of ancient fast food? | He says yes, comparing the bread, cheese, and related foods to quick Middle Eastern fare. | He says this kind of meal still resembles Middle Eastern fast food: a quick platter of cheese, flatbread, olives, and similar foods. He treats Abraham's hospitality meal as part of that older food pattern rather than a modern formal meal category. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=7740" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:00</a> | What exactly were the oaks of Mamre? | He says they were remembered landmark trees or a grove near Hebron. | He explains the oaks as a sacred oak grove, drawing on Old Testament references to worship around trees. He calls this a pre-Sinai exemption: such groves could function as holy places for the patriarchs, even though later Israelite religion would reject many tree-grove practices because of pagan associations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=7843" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:43</a> | If God was there, why did Abraham not experience the trauma of overwhelming holiness? | He says the encounter is gracious and mediated, not an unleashed judgment-presence. | He says the text does not explain exactly why Abraham was not overwhelmed, but Abraham clearly recognized the Lord and responded with extraordinary hospitality. He suggests divine or angelic beings can "tone down" their holiness, appearing with enough glory to signal a theophany but not enough to destroy or terrify the human recipient. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=7923" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:03</a> | Did the cherubim not look weird? | He says no in this scene, because they could take on human-looking similacrum bodies. | He says these beings can take on simulacrum bodies and look human if they choose. Such bodies are not permanent flesh-and-blood bodies with DNA or reproductive capacity, but they can appear human, behave human in some ways, and even eat. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=8002" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:22</a> | Why did the divine visitors accept Abraham's food if they did not need to eat? | He says Abraham offered hospitality and they accepted it, establishing the relationship within an ancient Near Eastern hospitality pattern. | He says they did not need to eat, but accepting the meal honored Abraham's hospitality and established relationship. He ties this to a broader ancient Near Eastern hospitality pattern and notes that refusing hospitality would have been perceived as an insult. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=8128" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:28</a> | Are any of the oaks still there? | He says no ancient survivor can be identified with confidence. | He says only a few oaks remain in the Levant and most were cut down for lumber over time. He explains that oaks were treated as non-fruitbearing trees and therefore were easier targets for cutting, including during Israelite warfare and settlement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=8238" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:18</a> | Has the climate changed too much to restore Israel's old forests? | He says restoration would be difficult, though not conceptually impossible. | He says reforestation might be possible if sustained long enough to create a local microclimate, but it would take a very long time. He notes that attempts to reforest Israel have been going on since the 1940s with mixed success, and some places have taken hold better than others. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=8320" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:40</a> | Were the plagues an attack on Egyptian mythos? | He says no; he thinks that reading breaks down, though the plagues still carry cultural messages Egyptians would have understood. | He rejects the idea that the plagues map cleanly onto attacks on the Egyptian pantheon, saying that view breaks down under scrutiny. He still sees cultural messages in the plagues, especially about Egyptian decadence and failure to live up to their own ideals, but not a simple god-by-god mythological attack. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=8451" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:51</a> | How is the proposal or book going? | He says it is moving, but slowly. | He says he is not actively working on it at that moment because another promised project has priority until around mid-September. After that, he expects to return to the publisher's proposal process, including another requested sample chapter, but no promise to publish has been made. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=8552" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:32</a> | What is the theology of original sin, and are we guilty for Adam's sin? | He explains inherited debt under the old covenant and contrasts it with the new covenant's different framework. | He distinguishes a genetic view, where sin is passed through descent, from a federal view, where Adam represented humanity as its priestly head. In the federal view, Adam's sin becomes a debt passed to his descendants, who add to it, while the new covenant changes the framework so each person is responsible for his own sin or hands it to Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=8948" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:08</a> | What change did Ezekiel 18 bring? | He says texts like Ezekiel 18 and Jeremiah 31 anticipate the move toward each person bearing responsibility for his own sin. | He points especially to Jeremiah 31's image of fathers eating sour grapes and children feeling the effect, then says the new covenant promises a change: each person dies for his own iniquity. He treats Ezekiel 18 and Jeremiah 31 as part of the shift away from inherited covenant debt toward individual responsibility. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=9339" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:39</a> | Would a bad smell in the temple be considered profaning it? | He says no; the sacrifices were necessary for covering sin, and washing and purification did not remove every practical smell. | He says the priests washed and purified constantly, but sacrifices involved slaughtering, cooking, and burning animals, so unpleasant smells were unavoidable. The sacrifices were not profaning the temple because they were the appointed means of covering sin and delaying judgment under the old covenant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=9573" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:33</a> | What are your thoughts on the Nestorian Stele and early Chinese Christianity? | He thinks the stele is likely legitimate and fits long-distance Christian trade presence in China. | He says Nestorian traders had long-distance routes reaching China and carrying goods along the Silk Road as far as Egypt. He thinks the stele is legitimate rather than a counterfeit, and he connects it with other indications, including Marco Polo's reports, that Christian or Christian-like communities reached China before him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=9722" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:02</a> | Was Emily Dickinson a Christian in your eyes? | He says he does not know enough about her life and work to answer confidently. | He says he cannot answer because he has not read much of Dickinson's work and does not know enough about her life. He uses the question to reiterate that this kind of classical literature and poetry is not an area he spends much time in. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=9885" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:45</a> | Why is Deuteronomy 32 such a bizarre chapter? | He says it is both text-critically messy and full of unusually strange imagery. | He says Deuteronomy 32 contains strange material such as dragons, divine allotment of land, and other unusual imagery. He also calls it a text-critical mess because the Masoretic Text, Samaritan Pentateuch, Septuagint, and Dead Sea Scroll evidence do not line up neatly, so good-faith scholars can disagree. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=10155" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:15</a> | Which is weirder, Deuteronomy 32 or 1 Samuel 13:1? | He says 1 Samuel 13:1 is chiefly a text problem, while Deuteronomy 32 is strange in its actual content. | He says 1 Samuel 13:1 is mainly a text-critical issue, while Deuteronomy 32 remains strange even when one focuses on the content. He points to its vivid phrases about blood of grapes, sacrifices to demons, fire reaching Sheol, and serpent or dragon imagery as examples of the chapter's weirdness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=10709" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:29</a> | Is there any archaeological evidence for the United Monarchy? | He says yes, though not in huge quantity, and points to evidence such as the Tel Dan stele. | He says there is some evidence, including the Tel Dan Stele's reference to the house of David and other evidence at the outer edges of the United Monarchy. He adds that most scholars now accept some form of united monarchy under Saul, David, Solomon, and briefly Rehoboam, though the evidence is not abundant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=10809" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:09</a> | Are there any good books, documentaries, or podcasts on the historical kingdoms of Judah and Israel? | He cannot recommend much off the top of his head, but mentions Thiele for chronology and gestures toward Mazar for related work. | He says he does not follow documentaries or podcasts closely and cannot name a focused book on the kingdoms off the top of his head. For chronology, he recommends Edwin Thiele's *The Mysterious Numbers of the Hebrew Kings*, especially the second or third editions, and tentatively gestures toward Eilat Mazar for related United Monarchy work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=10926" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:06</a> | What is the most reasonable cause of Jericho's walls falling? | He says the best natural explanation is that people weakened the casemate walls by hollowing them out and living inside them. | He explains Jericho's walls as casemate walls: two mudbrick walls with rubble fill between them. If residents removed the fill to make living space and cut windows into the walls, the structure would lose strength, so crowds pressing against weakened walls to watch Israel march could plausibly contribute to collapse. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=11209" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:49</a> | What is your view of God hardening Pharaoh's heart, and did he take away free will? | He says God confirms Pharaoh's repeated choices rather than removing Pharaoh's free will. | He says Pharaoh repeatedly hardens his own heart before God hardens it, so God is confirming choices Pharaoh has already made. He also notes Exodus uses several Hebrew terms with different nuances, such as strengthening, making severe, or making heavy, which he takes as God making Pharaoh more resolute in his chosen course rather than erasing free will. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=11507" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:47</a> | Did people in the ancient Near East steal sacralizing converters for the palladium? | He first reads it as a palladium cult-image reference, then recognizes the catalytic-converter wordplay and says he knows little about the ancient palladium tradition. | He initially looks up palladium as a cult image tied to the safety of Troy and Rome, then realizes the question is a joke about catalytic converters and the metal palladium. He says he knows the metal use better than the classical-antiquity tradition and does not claim detailed knowledge of the ancient palladium material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=11868" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:48</a> | What non-fiction books changed your life positively? | He highlights Kierkegaard's *The Problem of Being a Christian* and mentions an older small book on lordship theology as especially formative. | He mentions Mrs. Producer's appreciation for Wayne Grudem's systematic theology, then says a small, now-forgotten book on lordship theology changed how he understood salvation and the confession that Jesus is Lord. He also highlights Kierkegaard's *The Problem of Being a Christian* as influential, especially on being a Christian in conflict with church or public authority. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=12336" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:36</a> | Since Melchizedek seems to be a legitimate priest to God, can we assume God was relating to the people of Canaan for a long time? | He says yes in broad outline, especially before Amorite corruption brought later judgment. | He says God does seem to have been involved with Canaan in Abraham's time and perhaps earlier, with El Elyon language suggesting communion with some Canaanites. He then distinguishes that earlier pious patriarchal setting from the later Amorite takeover, political consolidation, and abominable practices that made their iniquity complete. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=12713" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:53</a> | Why are Ephraim and Manasseh considered Jewish if later Jewishness comes from the mother's side? | He says matrilineal Jewishness is a later rabbinic development; in Joseph's time lineage was patrilineal. | He says descent in ancient Israel was patrilineal, while matrilineal Jewish identity begins with rabbinic or Talmudic Judaism after the destruction of the temple and later diaspora conditions. Because paternal ancestry could become uncertain through displacement, enslavement, and forced relocation, the rabbis made the mother the sure line of Jewish identity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=13387" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:43:07</a> | Did you ever watch *Primeval*, especially the Egypt and time-travel episode? | Yes. He says he watched it and enjoyed it. | He says he did watch *Primeval*, including the episode with Egyptian hieroglyphs, idol statuary, time travel, and the alligator-god element. He says he loved the show, found the episode fun and crazy, and still watches the series occasionally. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=13466" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:44:26</a> | If people think Revelation has already been fulfilled, what is the current earthly nightmare? | He calls full preterism heretical and says Christ's return will be unmistakable and world-changing. | He rejects full preterism, saying it claims Revelation and Christ's return were fulfilled in AD 70 while the present world obviously does not look like the consummation of Christ's kingdom. He says the second coming is an essential Christian doctrine, Christ will return to judge the living and the dead, and everyone will know when it happens. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=14278" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:57:58</a> | Should we view secular liberalism as a Christian heresy? | He says secular liberalism is secular humanism, while Christian or religious liberalism is the actual heresy. | He distinguishes secular liberalism from Christian liberalism: secular liberalism may borrow Christian ethics from the surrounding culture, but it does not claim to be Christian. Christian liberalism is heretical in his view because it knowingly denies core Christian doctrines such as the virgin birth, resurrection, incarnation, and salvation by grace through faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=14654" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:04:14</a> | How would you respond to the claim that everything went south in the fourth century with Constantine? | He says Constantine gets blamed for myths and did not invent the canon or personally make Christianity the state religion. | He says the fourth century changed things, but it is false to blame Constantine for inventing the Bible, creating the canon, or making Christianity the state religion. He calls those claims repeated myths that historians have debunked and says they usually reveal historical ignorance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/NgKmadBOnGM?t=14743" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:05:43</a> | What is the fullness of the Gentiles? | He says it is the set number of Gentiles known only to God that precedes Israel's regrafting and the end-time sequence. | He explains the phrase through Romans' image of Gentiles being grafted onto Israel's tree while Israel is hardened for a time. The fullness of the Gentiles is the set number of Gentiles who must come to Christ; after that, Israel is regrafted, Jews come to Jesus, and the end follows. |
